(戦国時代, *Sengoku jidai*; Japanese pronunciation: [[seŋ.ɡo.kɯ (d)ʑiꜜ.dai, -ŋo.kɯ-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1) [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Warring States period') was the period in [Japanese history](/wiki/History_of_Japan "History of Japan") in which [civil wars](/wiki/Civil_wars "Civil wars") and social upheavals took place almost continuously in the 15th and 16th centuries. The [Kyōtoku incident](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dtoku_incident "Kyōtoku incident") (1454), [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War") (1467), or [Meiō incident](/wiki/Mei%C5%8D_incident "Meiō incident") (1493) are generally chosen as the period's start date, but there are many competing historiographies for its end date, ranging from 1568, the date of [Oda Nobunaga's march on Kyoto](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga#Ise_campaign,_Omi_campaign,_and_march_to_Kyoto "Oda Nobunaga"), to the suppression of the [Shimabara Rebellion](/wiki/Shimabara_Rebellion "Shimabara Rebellion") in 1638, deep into what was traditionally considered the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period").[[2]](#cite_note-jp191129-2)[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3) Regardless of the dates chosen, the Sengoku period overlaps substantially with the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period") (1336–1573).

The **Sengoku period** (戦国時代, *Sengoku jidai*; Japanese pronunciation: [[seŋ.ɡo.kɯ (d)ʑiꜜ.dai, -ŋo.kɯ-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1) [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Warring States period') was the period in [Japanese history](/wiki/History_of_Japan "History of Japan") in which [civil wars](/wiki/Civil_wars "Civil wars") and social upheavals took place almost continuously in the 15th and 16th centuries. The [Kyōtoku incident](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dtoku_incident "Kyōtoku incident") (1454), [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War") (1467), or [Meiō incident](/wiki/Mei%C5%8D_incident "Meiō incident") (1493) are generally chosen as the period's start date, but there are many competing historiographies for its end date, ranging from 1568, the date of [Oda Nobunaga's march on Kyoto](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga#Ise_campaign,_Omi_campaign,_and_march_to_Kyoto "Oda Nobunaga"), to the suppression of the [Shimabara Rebellion](/wiki/Shimabara_Rebellion "Shimabara Rebellion") in 1638, deep into what was traditionally considered the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period").[[2]](#cite_note-jp191129-2)[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3) Regardless of the dates chosen, the Sengoku period overlaps substantially with the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period") (1336–1573).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Sengoku_period_battle.jpg/250px-Sengoku_period_battle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sengoku_period_battle.jpg)

[Takeda Shingen](/wiki/Takeda_Shingen "Takeda Shingen") deflects [Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin")'s strike at the fourth [Battle of Kawanakajima](/wiki/Battles_of_Kawanakajima "Battles of Kawanakajima") during the Sengoku period

This period was characterized by the decline of the [Ashikaga shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate"), the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* central government, while the feudal lords (*[daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo")*), acquired greater political influence. The people rebelled against the feudal lords in revolts known as *[Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki")* (*[Ikkō-shū](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-sh%C5%AB "Ikkō-shū")* uprising).[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)

The period saw a breakdown in the traditional master-servant relationship between a lord and his vassals, with many instances of vassals rebelling against their lords, internal dynastic conflict over lordships within noble families (in which vassals would take sides), and the installation of figurehead lords by cadet branches of noble families.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The period was also marked by the loosening of samurai culture, with people born into other social strata sometimes making a name for themselves as warriors and thus becoming samurai. In turn, events sometimes allowed common [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") to rise to the rank of *sengoku daimyo*; these included [Hōjō Sōun](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_S%C5%8Dun "Hōjō Sōun") (the first to do so), and [Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin"), a *[shugodai](/wiki/Shugodai "Shugodai")* (deputy *[shugo](/wiki/Shugo "Shugo")*) who attained power by weakening and eventually replacing his lord.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-shugosen2-6) The most spectacular example of a *sengoku*-era rise is often considered to be that of [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), who rose from a peasant background to successively become a samurai, *sengoku daimyo*, and *[kampaku](/wiki/Sessho_and_Kampaku "Sessho and Kampaku")* (Imperial Regent).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Modern Japan recognizes [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"), [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), and [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") as the three "Great Unifiers" (三英傑／さんえいけつ) for their restoration of Japan's [central government](/wiki/Central_government "Central government").[[8]](#cite_note-denver-8)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

During this period, although the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") was officially the ruler of the state and every lord swore loyalty to him, he was largely a marginalized, ceremonial, and religious figure who delegated power to the *[shōgun](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun")*, a noble who was roughly equivalent to a [military dictator](/wiki/Military_dictatorship "Military dictatorship").

From 1346 to 1358, during the [Nanboku-cho period](/wiki/Nanboku-cho_period "Nanboku-cho period"), the Ashikaga shogunate gradually expanded the authority of the *[shugo](/wiki/Shugo "Shugo")*, the local military and police officials established by the [Kamakura shogunate](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate"), giving the *shugo* jurisdiction over land disputes between [*gokenin*](/wiki/Gokenin "Gokenin") and allowing the *shugo* to receive half of all taxes from the areas they controlled. The *Shugo* shared their newfound wealth with the local samurai, creating a hierarchical relationship between the *shugo* and the samurai, and the first early *[daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo")*, called *shugo daimyo*, appeared.[[9]](#cite_note-shugosen-9)

In 1428, [Ashikaga Yoshimochi](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshimochi "Ashikaga Yoshimochi"), the fourth Ashikaga shogun, was ill and the question of his succession arose. [Ashikaga Yoshikazu](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshikazu "Ashikaga Yoshikazu"), the 5th Ashikaga shogun, died of illness at the age of 19, so the 6th Ashikaga shogun was chosen from among Yoshimochi's four brothers, and to ensure fairness, a lottery was held. The sixth Ashikaga shogun was [Ashikaga Yoshinori](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshinori "Ashikaga Yoshinori"). He was not educated to be shogun, and his temperamental and despotic behaviour caused resentment. [Akamatsu Mitsusuke](/wiki/Akamatsu_Mitsusuke "Akamatsu Mitsusuke") assassinated him during the [Kakitsu Rebellion](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%98%89%E5%90%89%E3%81%AE%E4%B9%B1 "ja:嘉吉の乱"). This led to instability in the Ashikaga shogunate system. The shogunate gradually lost influence and control over the *daimyo*.[[10]](#cite_note-kyoto260523-10)[[11]](#cite_note-nagoyaka-11)

## Beginning of the Sengoku period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginning of the Sengoku period")]

Further information: [Kyōtoku incident](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dtoku_incident "Kyōtoku incident") and [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Onin-War-1467-1477-The-Battle-of-Onin-by-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png/250px-Onin-War-1467-1477-The-Battle-of-Onin-by-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png)](/wiki/File:Onin-War-1467-1477-The-Battle-of-Onin-by-Utagawa-Yoshitora.png)

19th century [ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") by [Utagawa Yoshitora](/wiki/Utagawa_Yoshitora "Utagawa Yoshitora"), depicting a battle of the war

The beginning of the Sengoku Period is considered to be the [Kyōtoku incident](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dtoku_incident "Kyōtoku incident"), [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War"), or [Meiō incident](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%98%8E%E5%BF%9C%E3%81%AE%E6%94%BF%E5%A4%89 "ja:明応の政変").[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)[[12]](#cite_note-kotomei-12)

The Kyōtoku Incident was a major war in the [Kanto region](/wiki/Kanto_region "Kanto region") that lasted from 1454 to 1482. The war began when [Ashikaga Shigeuji](/wiki/Ashikaga_Shigeuji "Ashikaga Shigeuji") of *[Kantō kubō](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_kub%C5%8D "Kantō kubō")*, the office of the Ashikaga shogunate in charge of the Kanto region, killed Uesugi Noritada of *Kantō [Kanrei](/wiki/Kanrei "Kanrei")*, Kantō kubō's assistant. The various forces in the Kanto region divided and fought between the *Kubō* and *Kanrei* sides, with the Ashikaga shogunate supporting the *Kanrei* side.[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)

[Ashikaga Yoshimasa](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshimasa "Ashikaga Yoshimasa"), the 8th Ashikaga shogun, tried to strengthen the power of the shogun, but his close associates did not follow his instructions, leading to political chaos and increasing social unrest. Since he had no sons, he tried to install his younger brother [Ashikaga Yoshimi](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshimi "Ashikaga Yoshimi") as the ninth shogun, but when his wife [Hino Tomiko](/wiki/Hino_Tomiko "Hino Tomiko") gave birth to [Ashikaga Yoshihisa](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshihisa "Ashikaga Yoshihisa"), a conflict arose among the *shugo daimyo* as to whether Yoshimi or Yoshihisa would be the next shogun. The [Hatakeyama](/wiki/Hatakeyama_clan "Hatakeyama clan") and [Shiba](/wiki/Shiba_clan "Shiba clan") clans were also divided into two opposing factions over succession within their own clans, and [Hosokawa Katsumoto](/wiki/Hosokawa_Katsumoto "Hosokawa Katsumoto") and [Yamana Sōzen](/wiki/Yamana_S%C5%8Dzen "Yamana Sōzen"), who were father-in-law and son-in-law, were politically at odds with each other.[[10]](#cite_note-kyoto260523-10)[[13]](#cite_note-nagoyao-13)

In 1467, these conflicts finally led to the [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War") (1467–1477) between the Eastern Army, led by Hosokawa Katsumoto and including [Hatakeyama Masanaga](/wiki/Hatakeyama_Masanaga "Hatakeyama Masanaga"), Shiba Yoshitoshi, and Ashikaga Yoshimi, and the Western Army, led by Yamana Sōzen and including Hatakeyama Yoshinari, Shiba Yoshikado, and Ashikaga Yoshihisa. In 1469, the war spread to the provinces, but in 1473, Hosokawa Katsumoto and Yamana Sōzen, the leaders of both armies, died. In 1477, the war ended when the western lords, including Hatakeyama Yoshinari and [Ōuchi Masahiro](/wiki/%C5%8Cuchi_Masahiro "Ōuchi Masahiro"), withdrew their armies from Kyoto.[[10]](#cite_note-kyoto260523-10)[[13]](#cite_note-nagoyao-13)

The war devastated two-thirds of Kyoto, destroying many aristocratic and samurai residences, [Shinto shrines](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine"), and Buddhist temples, and undermining the authority of the Ashikaga shoguns, greatly reducing their control over the various regions. The war caused disarray which rippled across Japan.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14) In addition to the military confrontations between separate states, there was also domestic fallout. In contempt of the shogunate, the daimyo who were subjected to remain in Kyoto instead returned to their [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Japan "Provinces of Japan"). Consequentially, some of these daimyo found that their designated retainers or *[shugodai](/wiki/Shugodai "Shugodai")*, representatives of their states appointed in a daimyo's absence, rose in power either to seize control of the domain or proclaim independence as a separate domain.[[15]](#cite_note-:1-15)

Thus began the Sengoku period, a period of civil war in which the *daimyo* of various regions fought to expand their own power.[[10]](#cite_note-kyoto260523-10)[[13]](#cite_note-nagoyao-13) *Daimyo* who became more powerful as the shogunate's control weakened were called *sengoku daimyo*, and they often came from *shugo daimyo*, *shugodai*, and *kokujin or kunibito* (国人, local masters). In other words, *sengoku daimyo* differed from *shugo daimyo* in that a *sengoku daimyo* was able to rule the region on his own, without being appointed by the shogun.[[9]](#cite_note-shugosen-9)

Historians often consider the Ōnin War, a ten-year conflict wrought by political turmoil, to be the trigger for what would come to be known as the Sengoku period. This [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war") would clearly reveal the Ashikaga shogunate's reduced authority over its shogunal administration, the provincial *daimyo* and Japan as a whole; thereby a wave of unbridled conflict would spread across Japan and consume the states in an age of war. Furthermore, weariness of war, socioeconomic unrest and poor treatment by aristocrats provoked the wrath of the peasant class. Farmers, craftsmen, merchants and even villages would organize uprisings (known as *"ikki")* against the ruling class. An extraordinary example is the [Kaga Rebellion](/wiki/Kaga_Rebellion "Kaga Rebellion"), in which the local *ikki* had staged a large-scale revolt with the support of the [True Pure Land sect](/wiki/True_Pure_Land_Sect "True Pure Land Sect") (thereby establishing the term *[ikkō ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki"))* and assumed control of the entire province of [Kaga](/wiki/Kaga_ikki "Kaga ikki").[[15]](#cite_note-:1-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) It is suggested by both scholars and authors that "*these succession disputes still might not have led to war were it not for the shōgun's lack of leadership*."[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)[[15]](#cite_note-:1-15)

### Start and end dates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Start and end dates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Oozutu.jpg/250px-Oozutu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oozutu.jpg)

*Ōzutsu* (Big Gun)

The [Kyōtoku incident](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dtoku_incident "Kyōtoku incident") in 1454, [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War") in 1467, or [Meiō incident](/wiki/Mei%C5%8D_incident "Meiō incident") in 1493 is usually considered the starting point of the Sengoku period.[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)[[12]](#cite_note-kotomei-12) There are several events which could be considered the end of it: Nobunaga's entry to [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") (1568)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMypaedia1996-17) or abolition of the [Muromachi shogunate](/wiki/Muromachi_shogunate "Muromachi shogunate") (1573)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHōfu-shi_Rekishi_Yōgo-shū-18) or entry into [Azuchi Castle](/wiki/Azuchi_Castle "Azuchi Castle") (1576), Hideyoshi's promulgation of the *[Sōbujirei](/w/index.php?title=S%C5%8Dbujirei&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sōbujirei (page does not exist)")* [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%83%A3%E7%84%A1%E4%BA%8B%E4%BB%A4 "ja:惣無事令")] law prohibiting war (1587), the [siege of Odawara (1590)](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)"), the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") (1600), the establishment of the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") (1603), the [siege of Osaka](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka") (1615), or the suppression of the [Shimabara Rebellion](/wiki/Shimabara_Rebellion "Shimabara Rebellion") (1638).[[2]](#cite_note-jp191129-2) The old, well-known definition is that the Onin War initiated the Sengoku period in 1467; and that it ended in 1568, when Oda Nobunaga entered Kyoto in support of [Ashikaga Yoshiaki](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiaki "Ashikaga Yoshiaki").[[2]](#cite_note-jp191129-2)[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)[[19]](#cite_note-kotosen-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Sekigaharascreen.jpg/250px-Sekigaharascreen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sekigaharascreen.jpg)

Japanese screen depicting the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") (1600)

However, even if 1568 is the end date of the Sengoku period, there are also various theories about the beginning and end dates of the following Azuchi-Momoyama period. The Azuchi-Momoyama period refers to the period when [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") and [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") were in power.[[20]](#cite_note-kotoazuchi-20) They and [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") are the three unifiers of Japan.[[8]](#cite_note-denver-8) The name "Azuchi-Momoyama" comes from the fact that Nobunaga's castle, [Azuchi Castle](/wiki/Azuchi_Castle "Azuchi Castle"), was located in [Azuchi, Shiga](/wiki/Azuchi,_Shiga "Azuchi, Shiga"), and [Fushimi Castle](/wiki/Fushimi_Castle "Fushimi Castle"), where Hideyoshi lived after his retirement, was located in Momoyama. The beginning date could be either when Oda Nobunaga entered Kyoto in 1568 to support Ashikaga Yoshiaki, or when Nobunaga expelled Ashikaga Yoshiaki from Kyoto in 1573 and destroyed the Muromachi Shogunate, or when Nobunaga moved to Azuchi Castle in 1576. It ended either when Toyotomi Hideyoshi died in 1598, or at the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600, or with the opening of the Tokugawa shogunate in 1603.[[20]](#cite_note-kotoazuchi-20)[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)

[Kaga ikki](/wiki/Kaga_ikki "Kaga ikki") occurred in 1488, [Hōjō Sōun](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_S%C5%8Dun "Hōjō Sōun") conquered [Izu province](/wiki/Izu_province "Izu province") in 1491, and in 1492 [Hosokawa Masamoto](/wiki/Hosokawa_Masamoto "Hosokawa Masamoto") banished the 10th shogun [Ashikaga Yoshitane](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshitane "Ashikaga Yoshitane") from Kyoto and installed [Ashikaga Yoshizumi](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshizumi "Ashikaga Yoshizumi") as the 11th shogun. Around this time, civil wars began to occur frequently throughout the country, and Buddhist temples in various regions grew as armed forces.[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)

### Kansai region and Kyoto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Kansai region and Kyoto")]

#### Creation of the puppet Ashikaga Shogunate by the Hosokawa clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Creation of the puppet Ashikaga Shogunate by the Hosokawa clan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Hosokawa_Masamoto.jpg/250px-Hosokawa_Masamoto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hosokawa_Masamoto.jpg)

[Hosokawa Masamoto](/wiki/Hosokawa_Masamoto "Hosokawa Masamoto")

[Ashikaga Yoshihisa](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshihisa "Ashikaga Yoshihisa"), who had become the ninth shogun during the Onin War, died at the age of 25, and Ashikaga Yoshitane became the 10th shogun. However, in 1493, Hosokawa Masamoto raised an army while shogun Yoshitane was away in Kyoto and installed the 11th shogun, Ashikaga Yoshizumi, in a de facto coup known as the [Meiō incident](/wiki/Mei%C5%8D_incident "Meiō incident") (明応の政変). Masamoto held the position of *[Kanrei](/wiki/Kanrei "Kanrei")*, second only to the shogun in the Ashikaga shogunate, and the equivalent of *[Shikken](/wiki/Shikken "Shikken")* in the Kamakura shogunate. This made the shogun a puppet of the [Hosokawa clan](/wiki/Hosokawa_clan "Hosokawa clan"), which served as the *Kanrei*. In recent years, it has been theorized that this incident marked the beginning of the Sengoku period.[[21]](#cite_note-meio-21)[[12]](#cite_note-kotomei-12)

Hosokawa Masamoto remained a bachelor for the rest of his life and adopted three people as his heirs. Following the advice of his vassals, Masamoto named [Hosokawa Sumimoto](/wiki/Hosokawa_Sumimoto "Hosokawa Sumimoto") as his successor instead of [Hosokawa Sumiyuki](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%B4%B0%E5%B7%9D%E6%BE%84%E4%B9%8B "ja:細川澄之"), who had been adopted first. As a result, Masamoto was killed by Sumiyuki in 1507. This incident is called *Eishō no sakuran* ([永正の錯乱](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B8%E6%AD%A3%E3%81%AE%E9%8C%AF%E4%B9%B1 "ja:永正の錯乱"), [Eishō](/wiki/Eish%C5%8D_(Muromachi_period) "Eishō (Muromachi period)") delirium). This triggered a struggle for the succession of the Hosokawa clan, which was divided into the Hosokawa Sumimoto faction and the [Hosokawa Takakuni](/wiki/Hosokawa_Takakuni "Hosokawa Takakuni") faction, and started a war called Ryō Hosokawa War ([両細川の乱](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B8%E6%AD%A3%E3%81%AE%E9%8C%AF%E4%B9%B1#両細川の乱 "ja:永正の錯乱")), which was won by Hosokawa Takakuni.[[22]](#cite_note-omono-22)

Hosokawa Takakuni installed [Ashikaga Yoshiharu](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiharu "Ashikaga Yoshiharu") as the 12th shogun. Meanwhile, [Hosokawa Harumoto](/wiki/Hosokawa_Harumoto "Hosokawa Harumoto"), son of Hosokawa Sumimoto, who had lost the war, collaborated with [Miyoshi Motonaga](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E5%85%83%E9%95%B7 "ja:三好元長") to defeat Takakuni at the Battle of Katsuragawa ([桂川の戦い](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%A1%82%E5%B7%9D%E3%81%AE%E6%88%A6%E3%81%84 "ja:桂川の戦い")) in 1527 and expel him from Kyoto. The authority of the *Kanrei* was thus destroyed, and with almost no support for Hosokawa Takakuni, he was forced to move from place to place. He gained the *sengoku daimyo* [Uragami Muramune](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B5%A6%E4%B8%8A%E6%9D%91%E5%AE%97 "ja:浦上村宗") as an ally and fought Hosokawa Harumoto in a war called *Daimotsu kuzure* ([大物崩れ](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E7%89%A9%E5%B4%A9%E3%82%8C "ja:大物崩れ")) in 1531, but was defeated.[[22]](#cite_note-omono-22)

Hosokawa Harumoto seized power, but he alienated Miyoshi Motonaga, who was his retainer but still held a strong position of power. Harumoto seduced the [Ikkō-shū](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-sh%C5%AB "Ikkō-shū") into a *[Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki")* against Motonaga, which resulted in Motonaga's death in 1532.[[22]](#cite_note-omono-22)

#### The rise of the Miyoshi clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The rise of the Miyoshi clan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E9%95%B7%E6%85%B6.jpg/250px-%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E9%95%B7%E6%85%B6.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E9%95%B7%E6%85%B6.jpg)

[Miyoshi Nagayoshi](/wiki/Miyoshi_Nagayoshi "Miyoshi Nagayoshi")

Miyoshi Motonaga's son, [Miyoshi Nagayoshi](/wiki/Miyoshi_Nagayoshi "Miyoshi Nagayoshi"), fought against Hosokawa Harumoto, but chose to subordinate himself to Harumoto. As a follower of Harumoto, Miyoshi Nagayoshi defeated [Kizawa Nagamasa](/wiki/Kizawa_Nagamasa "Kizawa Nagamasa"), the most powerful member of the [Hatakeyama clan](/wiki/Hatakeyama_clan "Hatakeyama clan") who served as *Kanrei*, and in 1547 defeated the 12th shogun, Ashikaga Yoshiharu and [Hosokawa Ujitusna](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%B4%B0%E5%B7%9D%E6%B0%8F%E7%B6%B1 "ja:細川氏綱"), son of Hosokawa Takakuni, in the Battle of Shari-ji ([舎利寺の戦い](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%88%8E%E5%88%A9%E5%AF%BA%E3%81%AE%E6%88%A6%E3%81%84 "ja:舎利寺の戦い")). This further reduced the power of the Ashikaga Shogunate. Miyoshi Nagayoshi was told by a retainer of the Hosokawa family that [Miyoshi Masanaga](/wiki/Miyoshi_Masanaga "Miyoshi Masanaga") had played a dark role in his father's death, and he petitioned his lord Hosokawa Harumoto to overthrow Masanaga, but this petition was not accepted, and, in turn, Nagayoshi was almost eliminated by Harumoto. In response, Miyoshi Nagayoshi attacked and defeated Miyoshi Masanaga, expelled Hosokawa Harumoto, Ashikaga Yoshiharu, the 12th shogun, and his son Ashikaga Yoshiteru from Kyoto, and established control over the Kyoto area in 1549. After that, he fought several times against Ashikaga Yoshiteru, who became the 13th shogun, for control of the Kyoto area. However, one by one, his sons died in war or from disease, and the [Miyoshi clan](/wiki/Miyoshi_clan "Miyoshi clan") began to decline rapidly.[[23]](#cite_note-nagayoshi-23)

By the time of the 13th shogun, [Ashikaga Yoshiteru](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiteru "Ashikaga Yoshiteru"), the shogun already had few direct fiefs and direct military forces, and his sphere of influence was limited to a few lands around Kyoto, losing both economic and military power. As a result, Ashikaga Yoshiteru was often chased out of Kyoto by the *sengoku daimyo* Miyoshi Nagayoshi and his forces, and was finally killed in an attack by the forces of [Miyoshi Yoshitsugu](/wiki/Miyoshi_Yoshitsugu "Miyoshi Yoshitsugu") and [Matsunaga Hisahide](/wiki/Matsunaga_Hisahide "Matsunaga Hisahide"). Ashikaga Yoshiteru was known as a great swordsman and was a student of [Tsukahara Bokuden](/wiki/Tsukahara_Bokuden "Tsukahara Bokuden"), who was known as one of the strongest swordsmen.[[24]](#cite_note-kotobateru-24) According to [Yagyū Munenori](/wiki/Yagy%C5%AB_Munenori "Yagyū Munenori"), a swordsmanship instructor in the Tokugawa Shogunate, Ashikaga Yoshiteru was one of the five best swordsmen of his time. According to several historical books, including [Luís Fróis](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Fr%C3%B3is "Luís Fróis")' *Historia de Japam*, he fought hard with *[naginata](/wiki/Naginata "Naginata")* and *[tachi](/wiki/Tachi "Tachi")* during a raid, defeating many of his enemies, but eventually ran out of strength and was killed.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The trio ([三好三人衆](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E4%B8%89%E4%BA%BA%E8%A1%86 "ja:三好三人衆")) of [Miyoshi Nagayasu](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E9%95%B7%E9%80%B8 "ja:三好長逸"), [Miyoshi Soui](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89%E5%A5%BD%E5%AE%97%E6%B8%AD "ja:三好宗渭"), and [Iwanari Tomomichi](/wiki/Iwanari_Tomomichi "Iwanari Tomomichi") supported the young head of the clan, Miyoshi Yoshitsugu, in leading the Miyoshi clan. However, after the assassination of the 13th Shogun, the trio fell out with another Miyoshi follower, Matsunaga Hisashige, over the 14th Shogun, [Ashikaga Yoshihide](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshihide "Ashikaga Yoshihide"), and war broke out. The latter three also came into conflict with Yoshitsugu. The Miyoshi regime virtually collapsed when [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") entered Kyoto in 1568. Miyoshi Yoshitugu and Matsunaga Hisahide submitted to Nobunaga, but were later killed by Nobunaga's forces. The trio was weakened and the Miyoshi clan declined.[[26]](#cite_note-kotobasan-26)

### Other regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other regions")]

#### Chūgoku region (Mōri Motonari)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Chūgoku region (Mōri Motonari)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Mori_Motonari.jpg/250px-Mori_Motonari.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mori_Motonari.jpg)

[Mōri Motonari](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Motonari "Mōri Motonari")

[Mōri Motonari](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Motonari "Mōri Motonari") was a *sengoku daimyo* who [pacified](/wiki/Pacification_(military_action) "Pacification (military action)") the [Chūgoku region](/wiki/Ch%C5%ABgoku_region "Chūgoku region") and is famous for his parable of the "Three Arrows", which explains the importance of clan unity. In his first battle, the [Battle of Arita-Nakaide](/wiki/Battle_of_Arita-Nakaide "Battle of Arita-Nakaide") in 1517, he defeated the overwhelming majority of the Aki-Takeda clan with a small force, which later became known as the "[Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama") in the West" as a battle in which a small force defeated a large army. Motonari became head of the [Mōri clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan") in 1523 at the age of 27. The [Amago](/wiki/Amago_clan "Amago clan") and [Ōuchi clans](/wiki/%C5%8Cuchi_clan "Ōuchi clan") were sharing power in the Chūgoku region at the time, and he switched the Mōri clan's allegiance from the Amago to the Ōuchi clan in 1525. Motonari destroyed the Takahashi clan by 1535 and ruled [Aki province](/wiki/Aki_province "Aki province"), [Iwami province](/wiki/Iwami_province "Iwami province"), and [Bingo province](/wiki/Bingo_province "Bingo province"), and destroyed the Aki-Takeda clan at the [siege of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Yoshida-K%C5%8Driyama_Castle "Siege of Yoshida-Kōriyama Castle") in 1541. Motonaga adopted his sons into the [Kikkawa clan](/wiki/Kikkawa_clan "Kikkawa clan") and [Kobayakawa clans](/wiki/Kobayakawa_clan "Kobayakawa clan") to expand the power of the Mōri clan, and the three clans cooperated with each other. In 1554, Motonaga became independent of the Ōuchi clan, and after inciting the Ōuchi clan to internal divisions through political maneuvering, he defeated [Sue Harukata](/wiki/Sue_Harukata "Sue Harukata"), who had been in control of the Ōuchi clan, at the [Battle of Itsukushima](/wiki/Battle_of_Itsukushima "Battle of Itsukushima") in 1555, and defeated [Ōuchi Yoshinaga](/wiki/%C5%8Cuchi_Yoshinaga "Ōuchi Yoshinaga") in 1557, destroying the Ōuchi clan and pacifying Nagato and Suou provinces.
Motonari destroyed the Amago clan at the [siege of Gassantoda Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Gassantoda_Castle "Siege of Gassantoda Castle") in 1567, and then pacified [Izumo](/wiki/Izumo_province "Izumo province"), [Oki](/wiki/Oki_province "Oki province"), and [Hōki provinces](/wiki/H%C5%8Dki_Province "Hōki Province"), thus pacifying the Chūgoku region, and later extended his power to parts of [Shikoku](/wiki/Shikoku "Shikoku"). He died in 1571 at the age of 75.[[27]](#cite_note-kotomori-27)[[28]](#cite_note-toukenmori-28)

#### Hokuriku, Kantō, and Chūbu regions (Uesugi Kenshin)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Hokuriku, Kantō, and Chūbu regions (Uesugi Kenshin)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Uesugi_Kenshin_Portrait_from_Uesugi_Shrine.png/250px-Uesugi_Kenshin_Portrait_from_Uesugi_Shrine.png)](/wiki/File:Uesugi_Kenshin_Portrait_from_Uesugi_Shrine.png)

[Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin")

In 1546, [Hōjō Ujiyasu](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_Ujiyasu "Hōjō Ujiyasu") defeated [Uesugi Tomosada](/wiki/Uesugi_Tomosada_(16th_century) "Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)") at the [siege of Kawagoe Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Kawagoe_Castle "Siege of Kawagoe Castle"), and the [Later Hōjō clan](/wiki/Later_H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Later Hōjō clan") established its power in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region").[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)

[Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin") (Nagao Kagetora) was a *sengoku daimyo* based in [Echigo Province](/wiki/Echigo_Province "Echigo Province") who fought various *sengoku daimyo* and increased his power through aggressive invasions. After unifying Echigo in 1551, he invaded the Kantō region several times from 1552 to 1569 and fought against Hōjō Ujiyasu. He also invaded the territory of [Takeda Shingen](/wiki/Takeda_Shingen "Takeda Shingen"), who ruled [Kai](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province") and [Shinano Provinces](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province") from 1553 to 1573, and fought in the [Battle of Kawanakajima](/wiki/Battle_of_Kawanakajima "Battle of Kawanakajima") five times between 1553 and 1564. In 1559, Kenshin had an audience with [Emperor Ōgimachi](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cgimachi "Emperor Ōgimachi") and the 13th Shogun, Ashikaga Yoshiteru. When [Imagawa Yoshimoto](/wiki/Imagawa_Yoshimoto "Imagawa Yoshimoto") was killed by [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga")'s forces at the [Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama") in 1560, the [Alliance Kai-Sagami-Suruga](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%94%B2%E7%9B%B8%E9%A7%BF%E4%B8%89%E5%9B%BD%E5%90%8C%E7%9B%9F "ja:甲相駿三国同盟") formed in 1554 between Takeda Shingen in Kai, Hojo Ujiyasu in [Sagami](/wiki/Sagami_Province "Sagami Province"), and Imagawa Yoshimoto in [Suruga](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province") was broken. Kenshin used this as an opportunity to seize Hojo Ujiyasu's territories one by one, and cornered the Later Hōjō clan at the [siege of Odawara](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1561) "Siege of Odawara (1561)") in 1561, but was unable to defeat them. On his return from the siege of Odawara, he performed a ceremony at the [Tsurugaoka Hachimangū](/wiki/Tsurugaoka_Hachimang%C5%AB "Tsurugaoka Hachimangū") and assumed the position of *[kantō kanrei](/wiki/Kanrei "Kanrei")*. Kenshin made peace with Hōjō Ujiyasu, who ceded part of his territory to him in 1569, and made Takeda Shingen a common enemy of Kenshin and Ujiyasu, but Shingen died of illness in 1573. After Takeda Shingen's death, he fell out with Oda Nobunaga and destroyed the Noto Hatakeyama clan, which was close to Nobunaga, at the [siege of Nanao](/wiki/Siege_of_Nanao "Siege of Nanao") in 1577, pacifying [Noto Province](/wiki/Noto_Province "Noto Province"). He then defeated Oda Nobunaga's forces at the [Battle of Tedorigawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Tedorigawa "Battle of Tedorigawa"). However, he died of a cerebral hemorrhage in 1578 at the young age of 49.[[29]](#cite_note-kotoken-29)[[30]](#cite_note-toukenken-30)

### Social changes with the advent of matchlock guns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Social changes with the advent of matchlock guns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Gunsmith_Storefront_in_Sakai_Osaka_by_Akisato_Rito_1796.jpg/250px-Gunsmith_Storefront_in_Sakai_Osaka_by_Akisato_Rito_1796.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gunsmith_Storefront_in_Sakai_Osaka_by_Akisato_Rito_1796.jpg)

Gunsmith storefront, [Sakai, Osaka](/wiki/Sakai,_Osaka "Sakai, Osaka")

When the Portuguese brought the [matchlock](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock") gun to Japan in 1543, it was mass-produced in Japan, and a gun called the *[tanegashima](/wiki/Tanegashima_(gun) "Tanegashima (gun)")* began to be used in wars. With the introduction of guns, a standing army of *[ashigaru](/wiki/Ashigaru "Ashigaru")* (foot soldier) became essential to victory in war, making it impossible for small local lords to remain independent, and lands were consolidated under *sengoku daimyo* with vast territories, and battles between *sengoku daimyo* became more intense.[[19]](#cite_note-kotosen-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/La_bataille_de_Nagashino_%28Mus%C3%A9e_Guimet_MNAAG%2C_Paris%29_%2839839578035%29.jpg/250px-La_bataille_de_Nagashino_%28Mus%C3%A9e_Guimet_MNAAG%2C_Paris%29_%2839839578035%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_bataille_de_Nagashino_(Mus%C3%A9e_Guimet_MNAAG,_Paris)_(39839578035).jpg)

The [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino") in 1575. Key to Oda's success during the battle was the deployment of 10,000 [Ashigaru](/wiki/Ashigaru "Ashigaru") arquebusiers.

During this period, the organized use of large numbers of *tanegashima* (guns) was essential to winning the war. In order for the *daimyo* to win the war, they had to secure a large number of gunsmiths and arms dealers, import large quantities of [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), the raw material for bullets, and [nitre](/wiki/Nitre "Nitre"), the raw material for gunpowder, conduct routine marksmanship training, and secure large quantities of materials for building war positions. It was Oda Nobunaga who did this most successfully.[[31]](#cite_note-proda2-31) He built Azuchi Castle at a strategic distribution point, brought several gunsmithing centers under his control, and established friendly relations with the Portuguese and merchants in [Sakai](/wiki/Sakai "Sakai"), which had become an international port. He examined the rice yields of the lands under his control and did not allow his retainers to take private ownership of the lands, leaving the management of the lands to his retainers. This made it possible to efficiently change territories according to the performance of the vassals, thus eliminating land disputes. In addition, he made it possible to form a standing army by assigning military service to each region according to rice production. He encouraged the economic activities of the common people. In this way, he rapidly increased his power.[[32]](#cite_note-proda3-32)[[33]](#cite_note-proda4-33)[[34]](#cite_note-proda5-34)

### Oda Nobunaga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Oda Nobunaga")]

Main articles: [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") and [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Azuchimomoyama-japan.png/250px-Azuchimomoyama-japan.png)](/wiki/File:Azuchimomoyama-japan.png)

Japan in the late 16th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/The_Three_Unifiers_of_Japan.jpg/250px-The_Three_Unifiers_of_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Three_Unifiers_of_Japan.jpg)

The three unifiers of Japan: from left to right: Oda Nobunaga, Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Tokugawa Ieyasu

In and around the [Kinai](/wiki/Kinai "Kinai"), the most politically important region in Japan, Oda Nobunaga allied with [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") to increase his power. Nobunaga defeated [Imagawa Yoshimoto](/wiki/Imagawa_Yoshimoto "Imagawa Yoshimoto") at the [Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama") in 1560 and moved to Kyoto in 1568 to support the 15th shogun, [Ashikaga Yoshiaki](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiaki "Ashikaga Yoshiaki"). Nobunaga defeated [Miyoshi Yoshitsugu](/wiki/Miyoshi_Yoshitsugu "Miyoshi Yoshitsugu") in 1569, laid [siege to Mount Hiei](/wiki/Siege_of_Mount_Hiei "Siege of Mount Hiei") in 1571, defeated [Asakura Yoshikage](/wiki/Asakura_Yoshikage "Asakura Yoshikage") at the [siege of Ichijōdani Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Ichij%C5%8Ddani_Castle "Siege of Ichijōdani Castle") in 1573, defeated [Asai Nagamasa](/wiki/Asai_Nagamasa "Asai Nagamasa") at the [siege of Odani Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Odani_Castle "Siege of Odani Castle") in the same year, and expelled Ashikaga Yoshiaki from Kyoto in 1573, thus destroying the Ashikaga shogunate. He overpowered the [Nagashima ikko ikki](/wiki/Sieges_of_Nagashima "Sieges of Nagashima") in 1574, defeated [Takeda Katsuyori](/wiki/Takeda_Katsuyori "Takeda Katsuyori") at the [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino") in 1575, and defeated [Ishiyama Hongan-ji](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji "Ishiyama Hongan-ji") in the [Ishiyama Hongan-ji War](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji_War "Ishiyama Hongan-ji War") in 1580. However, he was betrayed by his vassal [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide"), who drove him to suicide in the [Honnō-ji Incident](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident") of 1582.[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)[[35]](#cite_note-jkoda-35)

At the same time, the [Mōri clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan") overthrew the [Ouchi clan](/wiki/Ouchi_clan "Ouchi clan") in the [Chūgoku region](/wiki/Ch%C5%ABgoku_region "Chūgoku region"), and the [Shimazu](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan") and [Otomo clans](/wiki/Otomo_clan "Otomo clan") became major powers in [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu"). In this way, regional unification was promoted.[[3]](#cite_note-jk061222-3)

### Toyotomi (Hashiba) Hideyoshi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Toyotomi (Hashiba) Hideyoshi")]

Main articles: [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") and [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period")

Though a peasant by birth, [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") had risen through the ranks of *[ashigaru](/wiki/Ashigaru "Ashigaru")* (足軽, foot soldier), samurai, and *sengoku daimyo* under Nobunaga to become the most capable general of them all. When he learned that his lord Nobunaga had been killed by Akechi Mitsuhide, he immediately made peace with the Mōri clan, who were in the midst of a battle, and turned his army back faster than anyone could have predicted, defeating Akechi Mitsuhide at the [Battle of Yamazaki](/wiki/Battle_of_Yamazaki "Battle of Yamazaki"). Hideyoshi avenged his lord only 11 days after Nobunaga's death. The men who had been Nobunaga's chief vassals discussed future policy at the [Kiyosu Conference](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B8%85%E9%A0%88%E4%BC%9A%E8%AD%B0 "ja:清須会議"), and Hideyoshi began his path to becoming Nobunaga's successor. In 1582, Hideyoshi defeated [Shibata Katsuie](/wiki/Shibata_Katsuie "Shibata Katsuie") and [Oda Nobutaka](/wiki/Oda_Nobutaka "Oda Nobutaka"), who had been enemies over Nobunaga's succession, at the [Battle of Shizugatake](/wiki/Battle_of_Shizugatake "Battle of Shizugatake"), and in 1583 he began construction of [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"). In 1584, he fought bitterly against the allied forces of Tokugawa Ieyasu and [Oda Nobuo](/wiki/Oda_Nobuo "Oda Nobuo") at the [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute"), but was able to make a truce with Nobuo by making peace with him. In 1585, he defeated [Chōsokabe Motochika](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsokabe_Motochika "Chōsokabe Motochika") in an [Invasion of Shikoku](/wiki/Invasion_of_Shikoku_(1585) "Invasion of Shikoku (1585)") and pacified [Shikoku](/wiki/Shikoku "Shikoku"). In 1586, he became *[Kampaku](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku")* (Imperial Regent) and *[Daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin")* (Chancellor of the Realm) for the first time in history, although he was not a native-born aristocrat. In 1586, he also succeeded in getting Ieyasu to swear allegiance to him. In 1587, he defeated the [Shimazu clan](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan") in a [Kyūshū campaign](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB_campaign "Kyūshū campaign") and pacified [Kyūshū](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB "Kyūshū"). In 1590, he defeated the Later Hōjō clan in the [siege of Odawara](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)") and pacified the Kantō region. In the same year, he forced the clans of the [Tōhoku region](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region") to swear allegiance to him and finally achieved the unification of Japan.[[36]](#cite_note-jktoyo-36)[[37]](#cite_note-kototoyooda-37)

#### Tōhoku region (Date Masamune)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tōhoku region (Date Masamune)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Date_Masamune02.jpg/250px-Date_Masamune02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Date_Masamune02.jpg)

[Date Masamune](/wiki/Date_Masamune "Date Masamune")

[Date Masamune](/wiki/Date_Masamune "Date Masamune") was a one-eyed warlord, a famous *sengoku daimyo* who is often said to have had the potential to unite the country if only he had been born 20 years earlier.[[38]](#cite_note-toukendate-38)[[39]](#cite_note-asahidate-39) He became the head of the [Date clan](/wiki/Date_clan "Date clan") in 1584, two years after the death of Oda Nobunaga, destroyed the [Nihonmatsu clan](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nihonmatsu_clan "ja:Nihonmatsu clan") and other clans, and then in 1589, at the [Battle of Suriagehara](/wiki/Battle_of_Suriagehara "Battle of Suriagehara"), defeated the [Ashina clan](/wiki/Ashina_clan_(Japan) "Ashina clan (Japan)") to conquer the [Aizu province](/wiki/Aizu_province "Aizu province"), and continued to expand his territory to conquer most of the [Tōhoku region](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region"). On the other hand, Toyotomi Hideyoshi, who had only the Kantō and Tōhoku regions left to unify Japan, enacted a law called the *Sōbujirei* (惣無事令) in 1587, which prohibited *sengoku daimyo* from waging war against each other, and Masamune's conquest of the Tōhoku region was a serious violation of this law. After destroying the Later Hōjō clan at the siege of Odawara, Hideyoshi wanted to destroy the Date clan and other *sengoku daimyo* in the Tōhoku region who were reluctant to show their deference. Hideyoshi had his subordinate [Maeda Toshiie](/wiki/Maeda_Toshiie "Maeda Toshiie") question Masamune, who had arrived late to give the order to participate in the siege of Odawara, but after hearing Masamune's bold attitude, he decided to meet with him. Masamune showed his reverence by appearing before Hideyoshi in a pure white death robe, ready to be executed. Hideyoshi placed his staff on Masamune's neck and said, "If you had come a little later, you would have been beheaded," and Masamune pledged his reverence to Hideyoshi. He did not lose his life, only some of his territory was confiscated. He was later interrogated by Hideyoshi on suspicion of inciting a peasant uprising and participating in the rebellion of [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu"), but he defended himself with his usual courage and dignity and was not punished.[[38]](#cite_note-toukendate-38)[[40]](#cite_note-kotodate-40)

He was on the side of Tokugawa Ieyasu at the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara"), and as the first lord of the [Sendai Domain](/wiki/Sendai_Domain "Sendai Domain") during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), he developed [Sendai](/wiki/Sendai "Sendai") and laid the foundations of the city as the center of the present-day Tōhoku region. In 1613, he sent [Hasekura Tsunenaga](/wiki/Hasekura_Tsunenaga "Hasekura Tsunenaga") as an ambassador to Europe, where he was granted an audience with the [Pope Paul V](/wiki/Pope_Paul_V "Pope Paul V").[[38]](#cite_note-toukendate-38)[[40]](#cite_note-kotodate-40)

## *Gekokujō*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sengoku_period&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Gekokujō")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Map_Japan_Genki1-en.svg/500px-Map_Japan_Genki1-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Japan_Genki1-en.svg)

Japan in 1570

The upheaval resulted in the further weakening of central authority, and throughout Japan, regional lords, called *[daimyōs](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")*, rose to fill the vacuum. In the course of this power shift, well-established [clans](/wiki/Japanese_clans "Japanese clans") such as the [Takeda](/wiki/Takeda_family "Takeda family") and the [Imagawa](/wiki/Imagawa_family "Imagawa family"), who had ruled under the authority of both the Kamakura and Muromachi *bakufu*, were able to expand their spheres of influence. There were many, however, whose positions eroded and were eventually usurped by more capable underlings. This phenomenon of social meritocracy, in which capable subordinates rejected the status quo and forcefully overthrew an emancipated aristocracy, became known as *[gekokujō](/wiki/Gekokuj%C5%8D "Gekokujō")*, which means "low conquers high".[[41]](#cite_note-enc-41)

One of the earliest instances of this was [Hōjō Sōun](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_S%C5%8Dun "Hōjō Sōun"), who rose from relatively humble origins and eventually seized power in [Izu Province](/wiki/Izu_Province "Izu Province") in 1493. Building on the accomplishments of Sōun, the [Hōjō clan](/wiki/Later_H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Later Hōjō clan") remained a major power in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region") until its subjugation by [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") late in the Sengoku period. Other notable examples include the supplanting of the [Hosokawa clan](/wiki/Hosokawa_clan "Hosokawa clan") by the [Miyoshi](/wiki/Miyoshi_clan "Miyoshi clan"), the [Toki](/wiki/Toki_clan "Toki clan") by the [Saitō](/wiki/Sait%C5%8D_clan "Saitō clan"), and the [Shiba clan](/wiki/Shiba_clan "Shiba clan") by the [Oda clan](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan"), which was in turn replaced by its underling, Toyotomi Hideyoshi, a son of a peasant with no family name.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Well-organized religious groups also gained political power at this time by uniting farmers in resistance and rebellion against the rule of the *daimyōs*. The monks of the [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") [True Pure Land](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū") sect formed numerous *[Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki")*, the most successful of which, in [Kaga Province](/wiki/Kaga_Province "Kaga Province"), remained independent for nearly 100 years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
